topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom hummed with the forced gaiety of a hundred well-meaning acquaintances. I leaned against a marble pillar, nursing a lukewarm glass of sparkling cider, watching the swirling couples. Then, across the room, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was Sarah. She was laughing, her hair catching the light in a way that still made my chest tighten just a fraction. I took a slow, deliberate breath, letting the sensation settle in my shoulders rather than letting it spike. I watched her move, a quiet observation, allowing the moment to unfold without any sudden urge to intervene or retreat. When our eyes met across the crowded floor, there was a flicker—a brief acknowledgment of shared history—but neither of us moved to bridge the distance. I simply nodded once, a small, contained gesture, and turned my attention back to the intricate floral arrangements, focusing on the texture of the velvet drapes instead of the space between us.

[story 2]

I had settled into the corner near the buffet, strategically placing myself where I could observe the festivities without being directly in the path of the main traffic. The music was loud, the conversation relentless, but I kept my own internal rhythm steady, a deliberate, slow beat against the surrounding chaos. Then, there she was. Standing near the entrance, exchanging pleasantries with an older gentleman, was Alex. My gaze drifted over, registering the familiar lines around his eyes, the way he held his shoulders. A small, almost imperceptible tightening occurred in my jaw, quickly smoothed over. I didn't rush. I didn't search for an opening. I simply continued to sip my water, allowing the awareness of his presence to exist as a background noise, something to be processed slowly, without demanding an immediate response from me.

[story 3]

The air in the reception hall felt thick, saturated with perfume and champagne bubbles. I found a spot near the edge of the dance floor, choosing a vantage point that offered a clear view of the proceedings but kept me comfortably removed. My focus was internal, a quiet anchor against the external vibrancy. Then, I saw him. He was talking animatedly with a group of friends, his smile bright and easy. A strange, almost dull ache settled in my stomach, a familiar, low thrum. I watched him for a long stretch, letting the observation wash over me. I didn't fidget. I didn't check my phone. I just existed in that space, allowing the moment to stretch out, giving the encounter the time it required to simply be what it was—a fleeting intersection of two pasts—before deciding what to do next.
